quality.rationale: 纯理论证明题，逻辑链条完整涉及曲率平直、平行移动与坐标构造的数学物理结合。

origin.question: Problem 9.17. Show that the vanishing of the Riemann tensor is a sufficient condition for a spacetime to be Minkowskiian, i.e., a coordinate transformation will bring $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ into the form $\eta_{\mu \nu}$ .
origin.answer: 存在坐标变换 $\overline{x}^\mu = \Phi^{(\mu)}(x^\mu)$ 将度规化为闵可夫斯基形式 $\eta_{\mu\nu}$。
origin.solution:
Solution 9.17. Choose $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ to be mutually orthogonal at a point $\mathbf{P}$ and to satisfy $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A} = -1$ , $\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{B} = \mathbf{C} \cdot \mathbf{C} = \mathbf{D} \cdot \mathbf{D} = 1$ . Since spacetime isRiemann flat, parallel transport around a closed curve does not change a vector, so we can define A,B,C,D away from P by parallel transport.If we do this we have e.g.$$ rac {\partial \mathrm {A}_{a}}{\partial \mathrm {x}^{ eta}} = - \Gamma_{a  eta}^{\gamma} \mathrm {A}_{\gamma}$$therefore$$ rac {\partial \mathbf {A}_{a}}{\partial \mathbf {x}^{ eta}} =  rac {\partial \mathbf {A}_{a}}{\partial \mathbf {x}^{a}}$$so we can choose $\mathbf{A}_a = \phi_{,a}$ , and similarly for $\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}$ , and $\mathbf{D}$ . We can summarize by defining$$\mathbf {A} = \mathbf {W}^{(0)}, \quad \mathbf {B} = \mathbf {W}^{(1)}, \quad \mathbf {C} = \mathbf {W}^{(2)}, \quad \mathbf {D} = \mathbf {W}^{(3)}$$and we can define four functions $\Phi^{(0)},\Phi^{(1)},\Phi^{(2)},\Phi^{(3)}$ such that$$ oldsymbol {W}^{(\mu)} = 
abla \Phi^{(\mu)}.$$Since inner products are preserved by parallel transport$$\mathbf {W}^{(\mu)} \cdot \mathbf {W}^{(
u)} = [ \mathbf {W}^{(\mu)} \cdot \mathbf {W}^{(
u)} ]_{\mathbf {P}} = \eta^{\mu 
u},$$and this tells us that$$\mathbf {g}^{\mu 
u}  rac {\partial \Phi^{(\alpha)}}{\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mu}}  rac {\partial \Phi^{( eta)}}{\partial \mathbf {x}^{
u}} = \eta^{\alpha  eta}$$and hence there exists a coordinate transformation, namely$$\overline {{{\mathbf {x}}}}^{\mu} = \Phi^{(\mu)} ({\mathbf {x}}^{\mu})$$which takes the metric to the Minkowskian form.
